content.text: A neural vocoder is the component of a text-to-speech system responsible for converting acoustic features — typically an 80-band mel-spectrogram — into a raw PCM waveform. Before neural vocoders, classical signal-processing vocoders (Griffin-Lim, WORLD) produced perceptibly synthetic audio. Neural vocoders closed this quality gap by learning to hallucinate the fine-grained phase and harmonic structure of human speech directly from data, producing audio that fools human listeners in controlled tests.

Modern TTS pipelines split speech synthesis into two stages. The acoustic model converts text to a mel-spectrogram: a time-frequency representation with typically 80 mel-scale frequency bins at 12.5 ms frame steps (80 frames per second). The vocoder then inverts this compact representation back to full-bandwidth audio at 22,050 or 24,000 Hz.

This split exists because the mel-spectrogram is a lossy representation — it discards phase information, which is hard for models to predict but is required for waveform reconstruction. The vocoder learns to hallucinate a plausible phase signal consistent with the mel envelope. The quality ceiling is therefore partly set by the vocoder's ability to infer phase structure from amplitude-only spectral features.

attrs.alt: Neural vocoder pipeline: text → acoustic model → mel-spectrogram → HiFi-GAN generator with MRF → waveform; discriminators MPD and MSD used during training

content.text: WaveNet (van den Oord et al., 2016) introduced autoregressive waveform generation: the model predicts one audio sample at a time, conditioning each prediction on all previous samples via dilated causal convolutions. Dilations of
provide exponentially growing receptive fields, allowing the model to capture long-range temporal dependencies with relatively few layers.

The key limitation was speed: autoregressive generation at 24,000 Hz requires 24,000 forward passes per second of audio. Even with GPU parallelism, the causal dependency chain cannot be parallelized — sample

is available. Original WaveNet required several minutes of GPU compute to synthesise one second of audio. Parallel WaveNet (2017) addressed this with probability density distillation, achieving real-time synthesis at the cost of complex training.

WaveGlow (Prenger et al., 2019) replaced the autoregressive generation loop with normalizing flows. The model learns an invertible mapping between Gaussian noise and waveforms, conditioned on the mel-spectrogram. Because flows are fully invertible, training uses maximum likelihood directly. Synthesis draws noise from
and runs the inverse flow, producing the full waveform in a single parallel forward pass — solving the latency problem.

WaveGlow was real-time capable on a V100 GPU, though its audio quality was slightly below WaveNet and it required large model sizes (268M parameters). It was the primary high-quality vocoder in production TTS systems from 2019 to 2020.

HiFi-GAN (Kong et al., 2020) is the most widely deployed neural vocoder as of 2024. It uses a GAN architecture with a generator and two discriminator families trained adversarially, producing MOS scores comparable to ground-truth recordings while running faster than real-time on consumer hardware.
The generator takes the mel-spectrogram and upsamples it to waveform resolution through four transposed convolution stages. The total upsample factor is

, matching the ratio between waveform sample rate (22,050 Hz) and mel-frame rate (~43 Hz). After each upsampling stage, a Multi-Receptive Field Fusion (MRF) module processes the signal with three residual blocks in parallel — each using a different kernel size (3, 7, 11) and three dilation rates (1, 3, 5) — and sums their outputs. This gives the generator simultaneously narrow and wide temporal receptive fields, capturing both fine pitch cycles and longer phonemic transitions.
The MPD consists of five sub-discriminators operating at periods
. Each sub-discriminator reshapes the 1D waveform into a 2D grid of shape

and applies 2D convolutions. This allows the discriminator to assess periodicity at multiple time scales — fundamental frequency cycles (short periods) and syllable-level modulations (long periods). The prime period values ensure minimal aliasing between discriminators.

The MSD operates on the raw waveform at three scales: the original 1× signal, a 2× average-pooled (downsampled) version, and a 4× downsampled version. Each scale uses a 1D convolutional discriminator. The multi-scale setup captures both fine waveform structure and coarser envelope characteristics, complementing the MPD's periodicity focus.
The total generator loss combines three objectives:
: least-squares adversarial loss — generator fools both MPD and MSD.
: feature matching — L1 distance between discriminator intermediate feature maps of real vs. generated audio. Stabilises training and improves perceptual quality.
: mel-spectrogram L1 loss between generated and ground-truth audio. Ensures the output matches the conditioning input spectral envelope.

from transformers import SpeechT5Processor, SpeechT5ForTextToSpeech, SpeechT5HifiGan
from datasets import load_dataset
import torch
import soundfile as sf

processor = SpeechT5Processor.from_pretrained("microsoft/speecht5_tts")
acoustic_model = SpeechT5ForTextToSpeech.from_pretrained("microsoft/speecht5_tts")
vocoder = SpeechT5HifiGan.from_pretrained("microsoft/speecht5_hifigan")

# Load a speaker embedding (xvector from LibriTTS)
embeddings_dataset = load_dataset("Matthijs/cmu-arctic-xvectors", split="validation")
speaker_embeddings = torch.tensor(embeddings_dataset[7306]["xvector"]).unsqueeze(0)

text = "Neural vocoders produce remarkably natural speech by learning to invert mel-spectrograms into waveforms."
inputs = processor(text=text, return_tensors="pt")

# Acoustic model: text → mel-spectrogram
with torch.no_grad():
    speech = acoustic_model.generate_speech(
        inputs["input_ids"],
        speaker_embeddings=speaker_embeddings,
        vocoder=vocoder,   # HiFi-GAN inverts mel → waveform internally
    )

# speech is a 1D tensor of 16kHz audio samples
sf.write("output.wav", speech.numpy(), samplerate=16000)
If you have a mel-spectrogram tensor from any acoustic model, you can run HiFi-GAN directly to invert it to audio:

import torch
import numpy as np
import soundfile as sf
from transformers import SpeechT5HifiGan

# Load HiFi-GAN trained on LJ Speech (22050 Hz)
vocoder = SpeechT5HifiGan.from_pretrained("microsoft/speecht5_hifigan").to("cuda")
vocoder.eval()

# mel_spectrogram: tensor of shape [batch, mel_bins, time]
# For SpeechT5HifiGan: 80 mel bins, values normalized to ~[-11, 2]
mel_spectrogram = torch.randn(1, 80, 200).to("cuda")  # placeholder

with torch.no_grad():
    waveform = vocoder(mel_spectrogram)   # shape: [batch, 1, samples] or [batch, samples]

waveform_np = waveform.squeeze().cpu().numpy()
sf.write("inverted.wav", waveform_np, samplerate=16000)

BigVGAN (Lee et al., 2022) extends HiFi-GAN by replacing the MRF with Anti-Aliased Multi-Periodicity Composition (AMP) and using Snake activation functions — periodic activations that better model the sinusoidal structure of audio. Key improvements:
per channel, captures periodic structure without hand-designed receptive fields.
Anti-aliased processing at each upsampling stage prevents spectral artifacts from aliased convolutional outputs.
BigVGAN-v2 (2024) scales the generator to 200M parameters and achieves near-lossless inversion on out-of-distribution audio (singing, environmental sounds), not just clean studio speech.

# BigVGAN via HuggingFace Hub
from transformers import AutoModel
import torch

vocoder = AutoModel.from_pretrained(
    "nvidia/bigvgan_v2_22khz_80band_256x",
    trust_remote_code=True,
)
vocoder.eval().to("cuda")

# mel: [batch, 80, time_frames]
mel = torch.randn(1, 80, 300).to("cuda")
with torch.no_grad():
    audio = vocoder(mel)  # [batch, 1, samples] at 22050 Hz

DiffWave (Kong et al., 2020) and WaveGrad (Chen et al., 2021) apply score-based diffusion directly to waveform generation. They learn to reverse a gradual Gaussian noise corruption process, conditioned on the mel-spectrogram. Compared to GAN vocoders:
Vocoder quality is measured with both subjective and objective metrics:
MOS (Mean Opinion Score): human listeners rate naturalness on 1–5. Ground truth speech typically scores 4.5–4.7. HiFi-GAN V1 achieves ~4.4 MOS on LJ Speech.
PESQ (Perceptual Evaluation of Speech Quality): signal-level metric used in telephony; ranges from -0.5 to 4.5. Not fully correlated with MOS for neural vocoders.
STOI (Short-Time Objective Intelligibility): measures intelligibility rather than naturalness; ranges 0–1. Most modern vocoders exceed 0.95.
Mel-cepstral distortion (MCD): L2 distance between mel-cepstral coefficients of generated and reference. Lower is better; typical HiFi-GAN MCD is 1.5–2.5 dB.
Fine-tuning HiFi-GAN on a specific speaker's voice significantly improves naturalness for that voice. The standard setup uses 22–24 kHz audio, 80 mel bins, and trains for 500k–1M steps.

# Using the official HiFi-GAN repository
# git clone https://github.com/jik876/hifi-gan && cd hifi-gan

# config_v1.json (key parameters):
config = {
    "resblock": "1",             # ResBlock type (1 = larger, 2 = smaller)
    "upsample_rates": [8, 8, 4, 2],
    "upsample_kernel_sizes": [16, 16, 8, 4],
    "upsample_initial_channel": 512,
    "resblock_kernel_sizes": [3, 7, 11],
    "resblock_dilation_sizes": [[1,3,5],[1,3,5],[1,3,5]],
    "segment_size": 8192,        # audio segment per training step
    "num_mels": 80,
    "sampling_rate": 22050,
    "hop_size": 256,             # 256/22050 = 11.6 ms per frame
    "win_size": 1024,
    "fmin": 0,
    "fmax": 8000,
    "learning_rate": 0.0002,
    "adam_b1": 0.8,
    "adam_b2": 0.99,
    "batch_size": 16,
}

# Training command:
# python train.py \
#   --config config_v1.json \
#   --input_wavs_dir /path/to/speaker/wavs \
#   --input_mels_dir /path/to/precomputed/mels \
#   --checkpoint_path cp_hifigan

Training from scratch on a single speaker (1–10 hours of audio) converges in ~100k–200k steps and produces speaker-specific results that outperform the generic pretrained checkpoint. Fine-tuning the pretrained checkpoint converges faster — typically 50k steps is sufficient for a new speaker.

The mel-spectrogram is computed from raw waveform using a Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) followed by a mel filterbank. The STFT parameters directly affect vocoder quality because they determine what information is available for inversion.
Sets bandwidth ceiling; 22 kHz = 11 kHz max frequency
Upsample factor needed: 22050/43 ≈ 512. Smaller = more frames = slower acoustic model
Higher = more frequency detail retained, larger vocoder input
Sets mel filterbank range; fmax = sample_rate/2 captures full bandwidth
Mismatch between acoustic model and vocoder STFT parameters is a common source of quality degradation. The vocoder must be trained with the exact same
values as the acoustic model that generates the mel-spectrograms it will invert.

VITS (Variational Inference with adversarial learning for end-to-end Text-to-Speech, Kim et al. 2021) combines the acoustic model and vocoder into a single jointly trained model, eliminating the intermediate mel-spectrogram representation entirely. VITS uses a conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE) where:
The posterior encoder learns a latent z from raw waveform (via a linear spectrogram, not mel).
A text encoder and normalizing flow model z to the prior distribution conditioned on text.
A HiFi-GAN-based decoder generates waveforms directly from z. The HiFi-GAN generator IS the vocoder, but trained jointly with the acoustic encoder.

Joint training removes the error accumulation between stages: the vocoder adapts to the specific latent space produced by the acoustic model rather than inverting an independently defined mel-spectrogram. VITS achieves higher MOS than separate Tacotron 2 + HiFi-GAN pipelines on single-speaker benchmarks and is the basis of modern systems like Coqui XTTS.

# VITS via Coqui TTS (end-to-end, no separate vocoder step)
from TTS.api import TTS

# Downloads VITS model (acoustic model + vocoder as one)
tts = TTS(model_name="tts_models/en/ljspeech/vits")

# Single step: text → waveform directly (no explicit mel stage)
tts.tts_to_file(
    text="VITS combines acoustic model and vocoder into a single end-to-end system.",
    file_path="vits_output.wav",
)

A parallel trend replaces the mel-spectrogram with a discrete neural audio codec as the intermediate representation. EnCodec (Meta AI, 2022) uses a convolutional encoder to compress waveforms into discrete token sequences using residual vector quantization (RVQ). A decoder (analogous to a vocoder) reconstructs waveforms from these tokens.

In codec-based TTS systems (VALL-E, SoundStorm, Bark), the acoustic model is replaced by a language model that generates codec token sequences conditioned on text. The codec decoder then converts tokens to audio — no mel-spectrogram involved. This allows in-context voice cloning from a 3-second reference clip.

# EnCodec: compress audio to discrete tokens and reconstruct
from encodec import EncodecModel
from encodec.utils import convert_audio
import torchaudio
import torch

model = EncodecModel.encodec_model_24khz()  # 24 kHz, 75 tokens/s
model.set_target_bandwidth(6.0)             # 6 kbps = 8 codebooks × 1024 codes

wav, sr = torchaudio.load("speech.wav")
wav = convert_audio(wav, sr, model.sample_rate, model.channels)
wav = wav.unsqueeze(0)  # [1, channels, samples]

with torch.no_grad():
    encoded_frames = model.encode(wav)             # list of (codes, scale) per segment
    decoded_audio = model.decode(encoded_frames)   # reconstruct waveform

# codes shape: [batch, n_codebooks, time_tokens]
# time_tokens = audio_duration * 75 (75 tokens/second at 24kHz)

Neural vocoders invert mel-spectrograms to waveforms by learning to hallucinate phase information from amplitude-only spectral features. The vocoder quality ceiling determines the naturalness ceiling of the full TTS system.

HiFi-GAN is the dominant production vocoder: GAN-based, single-pass inference at >100× real-time, MOS ~4.4. Its MPD + MSD discriminator combination captures both periodic structure and multi-scale temporal patterns.
BigVGAN-v2 improves on HiFi-GAN with Snake activations and anti-aliasing, achieving near-lossless inversion on out-of-distribution audio. It is the state-of-the-art as of 2024.
Diffusion vocoders (DiffWave, WaveGrad) offer stable training and stochastic sampling but are 6–50× slower than GAN vocoders — better suited for offline high-quality synthesis than real-time TTS.
Fine-tuning a pretrained HiFi-GAN checkpoint on 1–10 hours of target-speaker audio (~50k steps) yields speaker-specific vocoders that substantially outperform the generic checkpoint for that voice.
